sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 65-year-old female, presents for follow-up after an inferior ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) where an additional stent was placed.
She reports experiencing similar symptoms to her previous heart attack, including chest pain that would not go away, which prompted her to visit the emergency room.
She has been experiencing lower extremity swelling since being discharged from the hospital and has noticed it is pitting 1+ on her right radial artery.
She has been taking her medications as prescribed, including aspirin, brilinta, and toprol, and has been monitoring her sugars with metformin.
She has not been adhering to a strict diet, particularly with regards to salt intake, and enjoys consuming high-sodium foods like pizza.
Past medical history:
The patient has a history of coronary artery disease, hypertension, and diabetes.
She has undergone an RCA stent placement in 2018 and was recently treated for an inferior STEMI.
Review of systems:
The patient denies any chest pain or shortness of breath, but has been experiencing lower extremity swelling.
Current medications:
Aspirin 81mg daily, Brilinta 90mg twice daily, Toprool 50mg daily, Metformin (no dose specified), Lipitor 80mg daily.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient's neck exam is unremarkable, with no jugular venous distention or carotid bruits.
The heart exam reveals a 3/6 systolic ejection murmur at the left base.
The lungs are clear, but the patient has lower extremity swelling with pitting 1+ on the right radial artery.
The cath site is clean, dry, and intact without hematoma, and the right radial artery pulses are palpable.
Test Results: The patient's EKG shows a normal sinus rhythm, good R wave progression, and evolutionary changes anticipated after a STEMI.
The echocardiogram shows a reduced ejection fraction of 35%.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's primary diagnosis is coronary artery disease, with a newly reduced left ventricular dysfunction and moderate mitral regurgitation.
The patient is also experiencing acute heart failure.
Problem list: Coronary artery disease; reduced left ventricular dysfunction; moderate mitral regurgitation; acute heart failure.
Plan:
I prescribed Lasix 40mg daily to manage fluid retention.
I also prescribed Aldactone 12.5mg daily to help with fluid management.
I referred the patient to cardiac rehab to educate her on regular exercise and improve her confidence.
I will continue her current medications, including aspirin, brilinta, toprol, and Lipitor.
I will also continue her Metformin.
I want to repeat an echo in about two months to monitor the patient's condition.
I recommend nutrition counseling to discuss alternative diet options and suggestions for reducing sodium intake, particularly with regards to preserved meats like pepperoni.
Follow-up: The patient will return in two months for a follow-up appointment to monitor her condition and assess the effectiveness of the treatment plan.
